title: Black Box Data After a Semi Crash in Jacksonville, Florida
twitter: 🚛 Hit by a semi in Jacksonville? The truck's BLACK BOX data can disappear in DAYS — not years. Don't wait. Call 855-WRECKMATCH (855-897-3256) now or visit https://www.wreckmatch.com/blog/black-box-data-after-a-semi-crash-in-jacksonville-florida before critical evidence is gone forever.
linkedin:
If your client was involved in a semi-truck crash in Jacksonville, Florida, the most powerful evidence in their case may already be at risk of disappearing.

Commercial trucks are equipped with Electronic Control Modules (ECM) and Electronic Logging Devices (ELD) — commonly called black boxes — that record speed, braking, hours of service, and driver behavior in the moments before a crash. This data is often automatically overwritten within days unless a formal legal hold is issued immediately.

Florida's 4-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims can create a false sense of security. The real deadline is far sooner. Attorneys who act within the first 24–72 hours are far better positioned to preserve this evidence and build a compelling case.

At WreckMatch, we connect injured victims with our network of 800+ vetted truck accident law firms across Florida and the nation — firms that understand evidence preservation, FMCSA regulations, and how to hold negligent carriers accountable.

If you or someone you know was hurt in a Jacksonville semi-truck crash, do not wait. The evidence won't.

📋 Submit a case now: https://www.wreckmatch.com/#form

Commercial trucks record everything: speed, braking, driver hours, and more. But that data gets overwritten fast. Florida gives you 4 years to file a lawsuit, but you may have only 48–72 hours to save the evidence that wins it.

reddit_title: Semi-truck crash in Jacksonville, FL — does the truck's black box data really disappear that fast?
reddit_body:
Yes, unfortunately it can disappear very quickly. Commercial trucks are equipped with an ECM (Engine Control Module) and ELD (Electronic Logging Device) that record critical pre-crash data like speed, hard braking events, throttle position, and driver hours of service. Many of these systems automatically overwrite data on a rolling basis — sometimes within 48 to 72 hours of a crash.

Florida's statute of limitations for personal injury is 4 years, which can give people a false sense of time. The real urgency is evidence preservation. An attorney can send a spoliation letter or litigation hold notice to the trucking company demanding they preserve all black box data, dashcam footage, maintenance logs, and driver qualification files. Without that letter sent fast, that evidence can legally disappear.

If you or someone you know was in a Jacksonville semi-truck crash, the best move is to contact a truck accident attorney within the first day or two — not weeks later. Resources like wreckmatch.com can help connect you with attorneys who specifically handle commercial truck cases and understand FMCSA regulations. Don't assume you have time — the clock on the evidence is already ticking.
